True Passive Voice (Η Παθητική Φωνή)

In Unit 18, we met Deponent Verbs (Type C). These are verbs that look passive (they end in -ομαι) but have an active meaning ("I come", "I need").

Now it is time to look at the True Passive Voice.

This is when you take a normal, active verb (like "I wash") and flip it around so that the subject is receiving the action ("I am washed").

1. Creating the Present Passive

How do you turn an Active verb into a Passive verb? You drop the active ending (-ω) and attach the passive ending (-ομαι)!

Let's use the verb πλένω (I wash).

  • Active: Πλένω το φλιτζάνι. (I wash the cup).
  • Passive: Το φλιτζάνι πλένεται. (The cup is washed).

You already learned these endings in Unit 18! Let's review them:

Pronoun The Passive Ending Example (πλένομαι - I am washed)
(Εγώ) -ομαι πλένομαι
(Εσύ) -εσαι πλένεσαι
(Αυτό/ή/ό) -εται πλένεται
(Εμείς) -όμαστε πλενόμαστε
(Εσείς) -εστε πλένεστε
(Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ονται πλένονται

[!TIP] The Reflexive Trick In Greek, the Passive Voice doesn't just mean an action is done to you by someone else. It is also used to show actions you do to yourself (Reflexive). - Πλένω το παιδί = I wash the child. (Active) - Πλένομαι = I wash myself / I take a bath. (Passive/Reflexive) - Σηκώνω το κουτί = I lift the box. (Active) - Σηκώνομαι = I lift myself / I stand up. (Passive/Reflexive)

2. Who did it? (The word "από")

When a sentence is passive ("The house was built"), you can choose to include the person who actually did the building ("The house was built by George").

In Greek, the word for "by" in a passive sentence is από (from). And as you learned in the last lesson, 'από' is always followed by the Accusative Case!

Το βιβλίο διαβάζεται από τον Γιώργο. (The book is read by George.)

3. Creating the Past Passive (Aorist)

Here is where things get completely new.

You know how to make an Active verb past tense (γράφω ➔ έγραψα). But how do you say "It was written"?

The Passive Aorist has its own unique set of rules and its own unique set of endings.

Rule 1: The Theta (Θ)

The hallmark of the Passive Past is the letter Θ (Theta). The vast majority of passive past verbs have a 'θ' right before the ending.

Rule 2: The Endings

The endings for the Passive Aorist are: -ηκα, -ηκες, -ηκε, -ήκαμε, -ήκατε, -ηκαν.

Let's look at the verb πλένω (I wash) in the Passive Past (I was washed). 1. Drop the -ω. (πλέν-) 2. Add the Theta. (πλέν-θ... wait, 'νθ' is hard to say. The 'ν' drops out! ➔ πλύθ- (Yes, sometimes the vowels change too!)) 3. Add the Augment (έ-) and the new endings!

Pronoun The Past Passive Ending Example (πλύθηκα - I was washed)
(Εγώ) -ηκα πλύθηκα
(Εσύ) -ηκες πλύθηκες
(Αυτό/ή/ό) -ηκε πλύθηκε
(Εμείς) -ήκαμε πλυθήκαμε
(Εσείς) -ήκατε πλυθήκατε
(Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ηκαν πλύθηκαν

(Note: These endings already start with an 'η' which is a vowel, so you only need to add an extra 'έ-' augment if the verb root itself is extremely short).

Ο καφές φτιάχτηκε από τη Μαρία. (The coffee was made by Maria.)

🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist

  • [ ] I know how to turn an Active verb (πλένω) into a Present Passive verb (πλένομαι).
  • [ ] I know that Passive verbs are also used reflexively (Things I do to myself, like wash/stand up).
  • [ ] I know that the agent (the person doing the action) is introduced by από.
  • [ ] I know that the Past Passive (Aorist) is characterized by the letter Θ and the endings -ηκα, -ηκες, -ηκε.
